APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it tackles open-domain QA over Wikipedia using a clear retrieval+reading pipeline, evaluates on standard benchmarks (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies) with accepted metrics (EM/F1), and compares to strong baselines (ElasticSearch API, BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention). The methodological choices (TF-IDF+bigram hashing retriever, 3-layer BiLSTM reader with attention and distant supervision) and reported hyperparameters match the community’s expectations for reproducible experimental work and systems-oriented contributions. The scope, datasets, and evaluation standards align well with the venue’s audience interested in scalable QA systems and rigorous empirical comparisons. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—open-domain question answering over Wikipedia—directly relevant to top-tier venues. The approach combines well-motivated retrieval and reading components, uses standard datasets (SQuAD, WebQuestions, CuratedTREC, WikiMovies), compares to appropriate baselines, and reports EM/F1 metrics expected by the community. Its use of distant supervision to train an end-to-end Wikipedia QA pipeline and thorough related-work grounding make it well positioned for the intended audience. Overall the scope, evaluation, and framing fit the standards of a top NLP/ML conference.

The methodological choices (TF-IDF+bigram hashing retriever, 3-layer BiLSTM reader with attention and distant supervision) and reported hyperparameters match the community’s expectations for reproducible experimental work and systems-oriented contributions. The scope, datasets, and evaluation standards align well with the venue’s audience interested in scalable QA systems and rigorous empirical comparisons.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (DrWiki architecture, TF–IDF + bigram hashing retriever, and multi-layer BiLSTM reader achieving strong SQuAD results). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported and related work is thorough, and figures/tables/examples appear effective for conveying results. A couple of presentation gaps remain—e.g., the distant-supervision paragraph filtering and discarded examples could be explained more transparently for reproducibility—so I rate clarity as very good but not flawless. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from problem motivation through a method that combines a retriever and reader, experiments across multiple datasets, and a conclusion; the main contributions are stated early (DrWiki architecture, TF–IDF+bigram retriever, strong Document Reader results, and distant supervision). Notation and model descriptions (3-layer BiLSTM, attention/aligned question embeddings, manual features) are consistently presented and hyperparameters are reported, and writing quality is generally good. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective, though the summary suggests some details of the distant-supervision filtering (paragraph discard criteria) could be explained more clearly for reproducibility. Overall the presentation is strong with only minor clarifications needed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (DrWiki architecture, TF–IDF + bigram hashing retriever, and multi-layer BiLSTM reader achieving strong SQuAD results). Notation and experimental details (including hyperparameters) are reported and related work is thorough, and figures/tables/examples appear effective for conveying results. A couple of presentation gaps remain—e.g., the distant-supervision paragraph filtering and discarded examples could be explained more transparently for reproducibility—so I rate clarity as very good but not flawless.

Notation and model descriptions (3-layer BiLSTM, attention/aligned question embeddings, manual features) are consistently presented and hyperparameters are reported, and writing quality is generally good. Overall the presentation is strong with only minor clarifications needed.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper tackles the central problem of open‑domain QA at scale and provides a practical, high‑performance pipeline (DrQA) that combines an efficient TF‑IDF + bigram hashing retriever with a neural Document Reader that achieved strong SQuAD results (70.0% EM, 79.0% F1 single model). The finding that a simple inverted‑index retriever outperforms the Wikipedia Search API and that distant supervision can effectively produce reader training data is likely to change how researchers build end‑to‑end QA systems and to be widely adopted and extended. By demonstrating a reproducible, scalable architecture and strong empirical gains on multiple QA collections, the work opens clear new directions for retrieval+reading hybrids and practical deployment. | B(5): This paper tackles the central open-domain QA problem with a practical, end-to-end system (DrQA) that combines a simple yet effective TF-IDF + bigram hashing retriever and a high-performing BiLSTM Document Reader, and demonstrates state-of-the-art SQuAD performance (70.0% EM, 79.0% F1). Its finding that lightweight non-ML retrieval plus distant-supervision for mapping diverse QA datasets to Wikipedia enables scalable open-domain QA is likely to change practitioner workflows, lower engineering barriers, and catalyze follow-up work on retrieval-reader pipelines and multi-dataset training. Given its clear, replicable methodology and demonstrated gains across realistic datasets (Wikipedia, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies), the contribution is broadly usable and opens substantive new directions for research and applications.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles the central problem of open‑domain QA at scale and provides a practical, high‑performance pipeline (DrQA) that combines an efficient TF‑IDF + bigram hashing retriever with a neural Document Reader that achieved strong SQuAD results (70.0% EM, 79.0% F1 single model). The finding that a simple inverted‑index retriever outperforms the Wikipedia Search API and that distant supervision can effectively produce reader training data is likely to change how researchers build end‑to‑end QA systems and to be widely adopted and extended. By demonstrating a reproducible, scalable architecture and strong empirical gains on multiple QA collections, the work opens clear new directions for retrieval+reading hybrids and practical deployment.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the central open-domain QA problem with a practical, end-to-end system (DrQA) that combines a simple yet effective TF-IDF + bigram hashing retriever and a high-performing BiLSTM Document Reader, and demonstrates state-of-the-art SQuAD performance (70.0% EM, 79.0% F1). Its finding that lightweight non-ML retrieval plus distant-supervision for mapping diverse QA datasets to Wikipedia enables scalable open-domain QA is likely to change practitioner workflows, lower engineering barriers, and catalyze follow-up work on retrieval-reader pipelines and multi-dataset training. Given its clear, replicable methodology and demonstrated gains across realistic datasets (Wikipedia, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies), the contribution is broadly usable and opens substantive new directions for research and applications.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear claims and supports them with experiments showing the TF-IDF + bigram retriever outperforms Wikipedia Search API and the BiLSTM reader achieves strong SQuAD numbers (70.0 EM / 79.0 F1). Mathematical content is minimal and appears sound; the methodology is generally well-specified (retriever design, reader architecture, hyperparameters). However, I see methodological gaps: no mention of released code or seeds for reproducibility, limited statistical analysis or variance estimates for reported metrics, and distant-supervision procedures discard many examples which could bias training—these do not overturn the main conclusions but weaken reproducibility and robustness claims. Overall the results are plausible and largely supported, but minor issues remain around experimental rigor and transparency. | B(4): The paper states clear claims and presents empirical evidence: a non-ML TF–IDF retriever with bigram hashing that outperforms the ElasticSearch Wikipedia API for article retrieval, and a 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader achieving strong SQuAD test results (70.0% EM, 79.0% F1). Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines (ElasticSearch, YodaQA, BiDAF, etc.) and use of distant supervision to map other QA datasets to Wikipedia paragraphs. Minor technical concerns remain — e.g., no reported statistical significance testing, and the distant supervision procedure discards examples that may bias training — but these do not undermine the main empirical conclusions, so I mark it as minor issues rather than fundamental flaws.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and supports them with experiments showing the TF-IDF + bigram retriever outperforms Wikipedia Search API and the BiLSTM reader achieves strong SQuAD numbers (70.0 EM / 79.0 F1). Mathematical content is minimal and appears sound; the methodology is generally well-specified (retriever design, reader architecture, hyperparameters). However, I see methodological gaps: no mention of released code or seeds for reproducibility, limited statistical analysis or variance estimates for reported metrics, and distant-supervision procedures discard many examples which could bias training—these do not overturn the main conclusions but weaken reproducibility and robustness claims. Overall the results are plausible and largely supported, but minor issues remain around experimental rigor and transparency.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and presents empirical evidence: a non-ML TF–IDF retriever with bigram hashing that outperforms the ElasticSearch Wikipedia API for article retrieval, and a 3-layer BiLSTM Document Reader achieving strong SQuAD test results (70.0% EM, 79.0% F1). Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines (ElasticSearch, YodaQA, BiDAF, etc.) and use of distant supervision to map other QA datasets to Wikipedia paragraphs. Minor technical concerns remain — e.g., no reported statistical significance testing, and the distant supervision procedure discards examples that may bias training — but these do not undermine the main empirical conclusions, so I mark it as minor issues rather than fundamental flaws.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides solid experimental evidence for its main claims: a clear retrieval comparison (TF-IDF + bigram hashing vs. Wikipedia Search API), strong SQuAD test results for the 3-layer BiLSTM Reader, and use of distant supervision to train end-to-end on open-domain datasets. However, important methodological details for full reproducibility and robustness are missing in the summary—e.g., no mention of release of code or random seeds, limited ablation analyses of the reader architecture and retrieval hyperparameters, and only partial reporting of failures (some distant-supervision discards). Overall the experiments are thorough and convincing but leave minor gaps in ablations, robustness checks, and reproducibility artifacts. | B(4): The paper runs comprehensive end-to-end experiments demonstrating both retrieval (TF-IDF + bigram hashing vs. ElasticSearch) and a strong Document Reader (3-layer BiLSTM) that achieves SQuAD state-of-the-art, and it evaluates multi-task training with distantly supervised paragraph mapping on multiple OAQA benchmarks. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, hyperparameters are provided, and practical limitations (dataset gap for CuratedTREC/WebQuestions/WikiMovies and paragraph-discarding in distant supervision) are explicitly discussed. However, the summary does not make clear whether thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of bigram hashing, manual features, retrieval depth) were performed, and code release is not mentioned, leaving some reproducibility and robustness questions unaddressed. Overall the empirical work is strong and convincing but has minor gaps in ablations and reproducibility details.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides solid experimental evidence for its main claims: a clear retrieval comparison (TF-IDF + bigram hashing vs. Wikipedia Search API), strong SQuAD test results for the 3-layer BiLSTM Reader, and use of distant supervision to train end-to-end on open-domain datasets. However, important methodological details for full reproducibility and robustness are missing in the summary—e.g., no mention of release of code or random seeds, limited ablation analyses of the reader architecture and retrieval hyperparameters, and only partial reporting of failures (some distant-supervision discards). Overall the experiments are thorough and convincing but leave minor gaps in ablations, robustness checks, and reproducibility artifacts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs comprehensive end-to-end experiments demonstrating both retrieval (TF-IDF + bigram hashing vs. ElasticSearch) and a strong Document Reader (3-layer BiLSTM) that achieves SQuAD state-of-the-art, and it evaluates multi-task training with distantly supervised paragraph mapping on multiple OAQA benchmarks. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, hyperparameters are provided, and practical limitations (dataset gap for CuratedTREC/WebQuestions/WikiMovies and paragraph-discarding in distant supervision) are explicitly discussed. However, the summary does not make clear whether thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of bigram hashing, manual features, retrieval depth) were performed, and code release is not mentioned, leaving some reproducibility and robustness questions unaddressed. Overall the empirical work is strong and convincing but has minor gaps in ablations and reproducibility details.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Given the summary, the paper does not invent fundamentally new learning algorithms but it presents a notable, practical combination and engineering of retrieval + neural reading with specific innovations (bigram hashing TF–IDF retriever, pragmatic distilled supervision mapping) that clearly differentiate it from prior QA work. From a methodology-focused vantage point this is a non-trivial, useful advance: assembling an efficient non-ML retriever that outperforms Wikipedia Search and pairing it with a high-performing multi-layer BiLSTM reader for end-to-end open-domain QA is informative to experts and not merely boilerplate. The contribution is more than incremental engineering yet stops short of a radical new paradigm, so I rate it as clearly novel but not groundbreaking. | B(4): The paper does not invent wholly new theoretical machinery but makes a notable, well-differentiated contribution by combining a simple inverted-index TF–IDF retriever (with pragmatic bigram hashing) and a high-performing BiLSTM Document Reader, and showing this pipeline attains SOTA QA performance on SQuAD. Its use of distant supervision to align diverse QA datasets to Wikipedia paragraphs and the empirical finding that a lightweight retriever outperforms the Wikipedia Search API are concrete, non-trivial advances over prior work. From an expert perspective this is a significant, practical step forward (clear differentiation and careful evaluation) rather than a purely novel theoretical breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: Given the summary, the paper does not invent fundamentally new learning algorithms but it presents a notable, practical combination and engineering of retrieval + neural reading with specific innovations (bigram hashing TF–IDF retriever, pragmatic distilled supervision mapping) that clearly differentiate it from prior QA work. From a methodology-focused vantage point this is a non-trivial, useful advance: assembling an efficient non-ML retriever that outperforms Wikipedia Search and pairing it with a high-performing multi-layer BiLSTM reader for end-to-end open-domain QA is informative to experts and not merely boilerplate. The contribution is more than incremental engineering yet stops short of a radical new paradigm, so I rate it as clearly novel but not groundbreaking.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper does not invent wholly new theoretical machinery but makes a notable, well-differentiated contribution by combining a simple inverted-index TF–IDF retriever (with pragmatic bigram hashing) and a high-performing BiLSTM Document Reader, and showing this pipeline attains SOTA QA performance on SQuAD. Its use of distant supervision to align diverse QA datasets to Wikipedia paragraphs and the empirical finding that a lightweight retriever outperforms the Wikipedia Search API are concrete, non-trivial advances over prior work. From an expert perspective this is a significant, practical step forward (clear differentiation and careful evaluation) rather than a purely novel theoretical breakthrough.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent QA models (BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention, Multi-Perspective Matching) and retrieval baselines (ElasticSearch, YodaQA) on standard datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies), using the same EM/F1 metrics. Comparisons appear largely fair, and the authors both report hyperparameters and acknowledge limits (e.g., distant supervision omissions and dataset-document gaps), but the evaluation lacks some deeper ablations and efficiency measurements that would clarify where gains come from (retriever vs. reader) and whether conditions match exactly across systems. Related work is discussed thoroughly, so I rate this as good comparisons with minor omissions in analysis and robustness checks. | B(5): The paper evaluates against a strong set of relevant baselines (ElasticSearch-based Wikipedia Search API, YodaQA, Dynamic Coattention Networks, BiDAF, Multi-Perspective Matching) and reports standard metrics (EM, F1) on the same datasets (SQuAD and open-domain QA collections). Comparisons appear fair and reproducible (same data and evaluation metrics), the authors explicitly acknowledge prior methods’ performance (e.g., state-of-the-art SQuAD numbers) and analyze differences between their TF–IDF + bigram retriever and search API as well as the BiLSTM reader architectures. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper discusses limitations of distant supervision, supporting a comprehensive and balanced comparative evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent QA models (BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention, Multi-Perspective Matching) and retrieval baselines (ElasticSearch, YodaQA) on standard datasets (SQuAD, CuratedTREC, WebQuestions, WikiMovies), using the same EM/F1 metrics. Comparisons appear largely fair, and the authors both report hyperparameters and acknowledge limits (e.g., distant supervision omissions and dataset-document gaps), but the evaluation lacks some deeper ablations and efficiency measurements that would clarify where gains come from (retriever vs. reader) and whether conditions match exactly across systems. Related work is discussed thoroughly, so I rate this as good comparisons with minor omissions in analysis and robustness checks.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against a strong set of relevant baselines (ElasticSearch-based Wikipedia Search API, YodaQA, Dynamic Coattention Networks, BiDAF, Multi-Perspective Matching) and reports standard metrics (EM, F1) on the same datasets (SQuAD and open-domain QA collections). Comparisons appear fair and reproducible (same data and evaluation metrics), the authors explicitly acknowledge prior methods’ performance (e.g., state-of-the-art SQuAD numbers) and analyze differences between their TF–IDF + bigram retriever and search API as well as the BiLSTM reader architectures. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper discusses limitations of distant supervision, supporting a comprehensive and balanced comparative evaluation.

main_claims: Propose DrWiki, a system for open-domain question answering on Wikipedia that combines a Document Retriever and a Document Reader.
A simple inverted-index TF-IDF retriever enhanced with bigram hashing outperforms the built-in ElasticSearch-based Wikipedia Search API for article retrieval.
The Document Reader (multi-layer BiLSTM model) achieves state-of-the-art results on the SQuAD benchmark (70.0% EM, 79.0% F1 on test for a single model).
Multitask learning using distantly supervised data to map existing QA datasets to Wikipedia paragraphs is an effective way to train a complete open-domain QA system.
_summary.methodology: Use an efficient non-ML Document Retriever (TF-IDF weighted bag-of-words with bigram hashing to 224 bins, returning top-5 articles) to select candidate Wikipedia articles, then apply a multi-layer (3-layer) bidirectional LSTM Document Reader with attention/aligned question embeddings and manual features to extract answer spans; augment training with distantly supervised paragraph extraction for datasets without paragraph annotations.
limitations: CuratedTREC, WebQuestions and WikiMovies do not include associated documents/paragraphs and therefore cannot directly train the Document Reader without distant supervision.
Paragraphs that do not contain exact answer matches or have insufficient overlap are discarded during distant supervision data generation (some examples are discarded).
Wikipedia is designed for humans, not machines, which poses challenges for machine reading (stated as a general challenge).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - open-domain question answering / machine comprehension

reviewer_a.name: Methodology-Focused ML Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior machine learning engineer with experience deploying and benchmarking large-scale NLP systems; past work includes reproducing and stress-testing open-domain QA pipelines and building retrieval/indexing components for production QA services.
traits: carefully inspects experimental design, including baselines, ablations, and control conditions
looks for thorough hyperparameter details, training procedures, and reproducibility artifacts (code, data splits, random seeds)
evaluates statistical rigor: sample sizes, variance estimates, significance testing, and robustness checks
examines efficiency and scalability metrics (indexing time, retrieval latency, model inference cost) and whether claims match measured resources
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, detail-oriented reviewer who emphasizes reproducibility and methodological completeness: I require clear baselines, ablation studies, precise experimental protocols, and evidence that results are robust and replicable.

reviewer_b.name: Domain/Impact Senior NLP Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Professor and researcher in natural language understanding with a focus on question answering and information retrieval; long track record of publishing and evaluating novel QA architectures and assessing their broader significance for the community.
traits: maps contributions to the existing literature and values clear articulation of novelty relative to prior work
considers broader impact: practicality for downstream applications, real-world constraints, and generality across languages/domains
notices the quality of evaluation datasets and whether experimental setup reflects realistic open-domain QA use-cases
values principled use of distant supervision and careful discussion of limitations and failure modes
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Strategic, context-aware reviewer who judges both technical novelty and real-world significance: I assess how the paper advances the field, clarifies trade-offs, and whether the proposed approach is a useful direction for future work.
